title: Modeling Project
MATH 435: Mathematical Imaging
text:
This repository contains the working course materials for MATH 435: Mathematical Imaging.
The course develops the mathematical foundations of imaging science from an inverse problems perspective. We will move from image formation and convolution to Fourier analysis, noise models, ill-posedness, regularization, variational models, optimization, sparse reconstruction, wavelets, and modern learned reconstruction methods.

title: MATH 435: Mathematical Imaging
text:
Current Focus

Build lecture slides first.
Keep Python examples close to the lectures.
Grow the online book from the stabilized slide material.
Publish everything from GitHub using Quarto and GitHub Pages.

title: MATH 435: Mathematical Imaging
text:
Course Arc

Images as mathematical objects and linear systems.
Blur, convolution, Fourier analysis, and noise.
Ill-posed inverse problems and regularization.
Variational imaging and optimization.
Sparse and multiscale representations.
Model-based, data-driven, and plug-and-play reconstruction.
Stability, robustness, ethics, and project defenses.

title: Syllabus Summary
text:
This course develops the mathematical foundations of imaging science from a modern inverse problems perspective. Students learn how images are formed, degraded, and reconstructed using tools from linear algebra, Fourier analysis, probability, variational modeling, and optimization.
The course emphasizes mathematical modeling, stability analysis, regularization techniques such as Tikhonov, Total Variation, and sparse methods, and computational implementation in Python.

title: Syllabus Summary
text:

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Today

What is an image mathematically?
Continuous images, discrete images, and pixels
Images as vectors
Imaging as a forward model
First computational representation in Python

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
What Is an Image?
At the mathematical level, an image is often modeled as a function

u : \Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}

where:

\Omega is the spatial domain
u(x,y) is the intensity at location (x,y)
color images use several channels, for example u : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}^3

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
From Continuous to Discrete
Digital images are sampled on a grid:

u_{i,j} \approx u(x_i, y_j)

For an image with m rows and n columns:

U =
\begin{bmatrix}
u_{1,1} & u_{1,2} & \cdots & u_{1,n} \\
u_{2,1} & u_{2,2} & \cdots & u_{2,n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
u_{m,1} & u_{m,2} & \cdots & u_{m,n}
\end{bmatrix}

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Pixel Grid

flowchart LR
  A["Continuous scene<br/>u(x,y)"] --> B["Sensor sampling"]
  B --> C["Pixel grid<br/>m x n array"]
  C --> D["Computation<br/>matrix/vector model"]

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
A First Forward Model
The central model of the course is:

y = A x + \eta

where:

x is the unknown clean image
A is the forward imaging operator
y is the observed data
\eta is noise or modeling error

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Examples of Forward Operators



Operator
Meaning




A = I
direct observation


convolution matrix
blur


subsampling matrix
missing pixels or lower resolution


Fourier sampling
MRI-style acquisition


projection operator
tomography

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Why This View Matters
Most imaging problems are inverse problems:

\text{given } y,\quad \text{recover } x

The naive answer would be:

x = A^{-1}y

But in real imaging, this often fails.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Why Inversion Fails
Three recurring difficulties:

A may not be invertible.
A may be badly conditioned.
Noise \eta may be amplified by inversion.

This is the reason regularization is not a technical detail. It is the heart of reconstruction.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Python Representation

import numpy as np

m, n = 4, 5
U = np.arange(m * n).reshape(m, n)

x = U.reshape(-1)
U_back = x.reshape(m, n)

print(U)
print(x)

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Row-Major Detail
NumPy stores arrays in row-major order by default:

U = np.array([[1, 2, 3],
              [4, 5, 6]])

print(U.reshape(-1))
# [1 2 3 4 5 6]

When implementing formulas, always state your vectorization convention.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
A Toy Masking Operator

import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 1, 12)
mask = np.array([1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1], dtype=bool)

y = x[mask]

print("unknown image vector:", x)
print("observed entries:", y)

This is a very simple forward model: only some pixels are observed.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
In-Class Question
Suppose A keeps only every other pixel.
What information has been lost?
What kinds of assumptions might help recover a plausible image?

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Modeling Habit
For every imaging problem, ask:

What is the unknown?
What is measured?
What is the forward operator?
What noise model is reasonable?
What prior knowledge should guide reconstruction?

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Takeaways

A digital image can be treated as both an array and a vector.
Image formation can often be written as y = Ax + \eta.
Reconstruction asks for x from incomplete, blurred, noisy, or indirect data.
The inverse problem viewpoint will organize the whole course.
